notes.note: The epikephalaia were head- or poll-taxes imposed on the provincials, here so heavy and the province so impoverished that the communities could not meet them. Cicero found Cilicia crushed by exactions levied under his predecessor and by the demands of the tax-farmers and creditors.

The Greek onai were the rights to collect or buy up taxes and other revenues, which the desperate cities had already sold off in advance to raise cash. The result was that future income was mortgaged and the communities left destitute.

notes.anchor: what is wont to be furnished under the Lex Julia
notes.note: Caesar's lex Julia de repetundis (59 BC) fixed strict limits on what a governor and his staff could requisition from a province, allowing such things as hay, firewood, and lodging. Cicero boasts of declining even these legal entitlements, accepting no more than four couches and a roof.

notes.anchor: our cavalry had been cut up by the barbarians
notes.note: Reports reaching Cicero said Roman horsemen had been destroyed by the Parthians, the great threat hanging over Cilicia and Syria in 51 BC after Crassus's defeat at Carrhae. The danger shaped Cicero's whole campaign on the Cappadocian and Syrian frontier.
